speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The first part of this interview was with Dick Benge. The second with his wife Ella Mae Arden Benge and her daughter Peggy Shott. And the interview took place at their home on Hattercreek near Princeton on April 27th, 1976.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How your parents raised you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I had one up at Hanover.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, how they raised you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I was raised in Nebraska on a farm. Well, you told me that, but I was wondering about how they did. I mean, what they wanted, whether they were strict with you or lenient, whether they made you toe the line. But, I had work. He read that. He wasn't very healthy.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Bell and Pat.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Them days they called it consumption.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, your old father, you know, he seen an advertisement in a paper and so he longed their mind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Long German. Made in Germany. Medicine. He had to send it to Germany to get it. We took that for six months. And the glass, everywhere he took it up to, in four weeks, it just crumbled that glass. So it must have been pretty powerful. Well, he got all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That did it for him? That cured him?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, it got him a lot better anyhow. So then his doctor told him, he said, we were down and he was talking to him and he said, I believe you ought to go get out of here. I said, you might be good for a long time yet. Well, he did. We come out here.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And that's how we come to come.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, Dad, he was very lenient. Of course he made me mind. Yes. I had work six days a week. I think I bet Saturday night and Sunday was mine. I had a horse to ride. Got a little older. I had a buggy. Got a brand new buggy for me and a good driving horse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what more could you ask?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Nowadays, they get them an automobile. What was the work that he was having you do when you were...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All farm work?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: All farm work. First part of farming.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I'll tell you, I'll give you the layout. He had ten milk cows. He had eleven broodmares. And he had fifteen sows for pigs. And he bred them all every year.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well... That's when the tractor first come out.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So... He had five good teams to sell. First, they run them one year. And the next year, they run them on the fence corner. And the next year, they all went back to horses and mules. Yeah, they wouldn't monkey the damn thing. They couldn't get them started with a cold morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said they run them in a fence corner? What do you mean?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: They just quit them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Nebraska?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Mm -hmm. Yeah, we was sixty -five miles south of Lincoln. We'd ride down the hall with only, what, about twelve or thirteen miles down the campus line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have many neighbors there, like here? Did you have many neighbors there?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, that was thickly settled, you know. There's a house on every well. It has section 160 acres anyhow.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So... I was back there last fall, a year ago. This fall. Well... Now there's a mile through there that there was... Well, I'll have to count up and see now. There's one, two... three, four... five, six, seven, eight houses at that time. Well, when I was back there, there was three.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: The house that we lived in when we left there. Nothing there. And a nice ten -inch orchard, it was gone. Nice maple grove, it was gone.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Barman! So, this house is set up on a little raise, that is. Well, there was a year they called it, I don't know what they would call it. Just kind of a level place. So, I just walked off over the hill where the house used to be from where it was. Just off over a little slope. I don't know how to say there, it's set at two million and a half dollars worth of farm machinery.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Corn planters, corn huskers. Everything. Five tractors. Wires.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No wonder the country's into it, is it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: no wonder they're gone belly up. This automobile's machinery's put them into it, too. Well, alright, we're put this way. You know, when I was growing up, we tried to get a hold of a piece of land or something. We'd get a job, but now the kids get a job, they stop on the road home after they get the job. Buy us a car.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the people there, your neighbors there, were they at all different? Was the neighborhood any different from the way it was over here?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yes, it was. There was more of a clam.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Over there? What was that, what was it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, they all had a clam. You know, an old one another, and, you know, neighbors back and forth. Go Saturday night a foot, see their neighbor, and stay till midnight, one o 'clock, have supper and play cards. Be sociable. No, they don't do that, you know. It was all sociable there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, you mean they were more, were they more that way over there than they were, say, like around here?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Of course, now this country here was goody that way when we come here, that is not so much as well back there. But, it's a safe one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I'm thinking, back in them days, I wonder why they were closer back there, maybe because they've been there longer?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I'll put it this way, the Minos and the different class of people. You know, more sociable. Want to get out, have a little fun, be sociable and not be out much.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Hum. Yeah, maybe I'm wrong, I don't know what that's way I always looked at it. Dad and I talked to over lots about the difference in the people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, there was mostly farmers there, right?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: All farmers. Nothing but farming there, and stock raising.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, maybe that had something to do with it too. Of course they all.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Every farm had a bunch of stock on it. Bunch of milk cows, bunch of brood matter, bunch of brood sows. Well, you know, that made the living. I know there was one time, I believe it was the year before we left back there.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: We had milk in the ten cows, churning the butter, feeding the buttermilk to the brood sows and little pigs. We were $274 ahead of the store that spring. When we started the farming. Well, $274 was pretty much through the summer. At that time, of course, we raised our eggs and butter and other milk and meat. But we went down to the store and got everything we wanted, clothes and all. In the summer. Yeah. So, you tell me somebody now is ahead of the storm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you sold out, did you get out ahead?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get much to bring over here to start out?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, I don't know. He had $10 ,000 to $12 ,000 when he got here. Well, it cost quite a bit. It cost us $150 for a car to ship out here on. We bring a team and wagon and a buggy and all of the house furniture and such as that with us. We didn't sell out there, you know. Yeah.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh. There's something the way this old world turns over, Rachie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The way it does what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Turns over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think about leaving at the time? Were you sorry to go?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No. No, I'll tell you. I was glad to get away for Dad's sake. The doctor told him he'd have to go and we know some folks lived here at Princeton. So, we rode up. Dave rode out to them and found out. So, they come. We come out. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it easy for him to start up farming here again?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, you know, it's comical. We went to potlatch and got a brand new plow, a walking plow. Half breaker, 12 inch. Corcoran had the little dream.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Dad started to plow and I looked and a little 12 inch plow passed. Dad come on around and I said, why God if that's the whole level turn over we never will get on. We used to them gangs and all that, you know. It took four, five, six feet at a shot. Now, in that way you'd have to make that many rounds for this one. But time did go so. But we didn't have much of plow. So.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that all you could use here? Is that all you could use here, a 12 inch plow?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, no, that's all I, we just had one team. We just had one team with us.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, that's why we got the 12 inch plow. Well, they're nice and stumps too. The light there wouldn't be in the light there nicely around the stumps. Of course, we went around and talked to all these fellas that had been raised around here and all. Found which would be the most suitable. For you know, we didn't know. We didn't know.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You know, he got to learn. He goes into a new country. He got to ask a few questions and learn a little. Who is right up to date.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. You know, I worked out somewhere, if we get to work down there, dollar a day. $25 a month on the board or dollar a day on the board. Or dollar, I mean, yeah, dollar a day on the board. They'd come out here and they'd give me a dollar and a half a day. But I thought I'd make money. Come find out, you know, it took all of her clothes from the back. That's actually 50 cents, just that much higher. I wouldn't add. Not a head at all. It was the same thing. Same thing. She did level it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you do most of the working out that you did when you did work out? Before you started in the woods, was it mostly like for harvesting?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, harvesting. Well, I worked out in the flat just outside of Francis -Napper. Worked for old. I worked for him. He'd give me a dollar and a half a day. God, I thought I'd make her. Come find out at the end of the month, I just swear, what was behind us, it would have been the brass getting a dollar or nothing. Yeah. I found out she was going to take a lot more money here than she did back there.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So then that year we took in harvest, I did. Two and a quarter days, boy, that was money. Jesus Christ. Well, that poor day light in the fall and the summertime and the threshing season. Stay with her till dark. Several hours. That's the hours that we got in and harvested.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And we was with Bain and McFarland and they were pretty light on them. A lot of them rode the whistle to go to work and we'd bow his whistle for us to get up. I don't know, he's a good threshing machine man and I got more threshing than the rest of them. The first year I was here, we caught in 65 days on that threshing machine. Boy, you get contoured to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, it was a good money. Well, it was good money then, you know. Jesus, the hours.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You must have been feeling like a dead dog at the end of the day. No. Get out. It's outdoors all the time, slept out all the night. You know, four or five hours sleeping out under the stars. Doing as much good as six or eight in the house. Yeah.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the crew working with all these other men there to harvest? Was that kind of fun?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh yeah. Oh yeah. That one morning, I forget, there was two sheep and John Burton and George Vassar and George wouldn't get up. John would try to get him up and he wouldn't get up. John said he'd come down by me one morning and he said I'm going to set that goddamn bed apart one of these mornings.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Next morning he got up and he said George get up. He said George get up. He said I told you the last time I'm going to set this goddamn bed apart and I'll bet you do get up. Next morning he did set it apart. He was in it. Half his bed and he just stuck his hands in his pockets and went down towards the cookhouse with whistler.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: George come out of it. It's his bed, half of it is his bed and half of it is John's bed.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, he was, they were the good outfit to work for. They didn't work like some of them. I mean that was the end of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He just jumped out and the bed burned up?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No. He beat her out. He had two dozen eggs. Yeah, they had straw under the bed. He took a quilt or a blanket or something and got a handful of work, one straw and rolled her up. Next morning he got up.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said goddamn son of a bitch you just what him and he eat up in the morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You get up in the morning. He did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you say some of these outfits really did work the man, eh?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, work them. Yeah, some of them in the fall. Rolled that whistle for them to go to work just in daylight. They'd work her just as long as they could see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they pay much more?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Two and a half a day for pitching. Boy, that was a hard pitching tooth because we went on up to the river. Most of us stacked green and boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hello, how are you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: That was a hard pitching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you been down to get your milk yet?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: We had a 36 -60 separator to try to keep us going. That old girl would eat a lot of grain and eat it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a cookhouse with you? Did you eat at the farmer's place?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: We had a cookhouse.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, we didn't have that one thing. Once in a while we couldn't get the cookhouse to the machine. We never had to go over 100 yards anyhow. I see. We don't have one jar here. That helps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or do we have two jars, baby?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I heard that women in the cookhouse worked plenty hard too. Oh my God, did they work. Yeah.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: The last two years, with me and my father and I, I moved the cookhouse, me and another.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: That's a long box.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Boy, they worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you want to do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I don't know. I could get a gallon of milk down there because we're using a hot gallon of wheat.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You won't have to rush to bring it out.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: It would be alright too, but that would be too true. You could just keep it down there. It'll come back from there. I don't know what you'd have for that. I'd have that for money. Fun.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, fine. Yeah, a lot of fun.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, I agree.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, she's getting ready to move, isn't she?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. But, they paid for it. Labor. Oh my God. You know, the labor man right now, they got the brothers working in the woods, wouldn't get out the way we worked. Forty dollars a day. No, they wouldn't.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, you know, they got to have forty, fifty, sixty dollars a day now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, there's no wonder our lumber and everything is going up, is it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I thought they were doing it. Labor is driving it up.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You know. Oh, you know, I'm not sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One thing I was going to ask you about along this line about working is, did you yourself have much ambition to be a farmer? Or did you prefer to work in the woods?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, you know, I liked woodworking better. Of course, a little more dangerous. You had to be watched, watch everything.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: But, when you're working in the woods, when you got your day in, you're doing.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: When I went to work, see, they'd work in ten hours. That was over the 1917 for the IWW struck. Well, they didn't strike for more money. All the trucks were in better conditions. And at that time, you know, we was all back in our own quilts, sugans we called them. And, boy, I was working in the camp, working in the kitchen. I come home one, I took two weeks off. I come home and I went back. Way late when I got in the camp that night and I had to walk. About seven, eight miles. Well, I pooped around down Bow Hill and got dark before I left there. And I heard some little motor running. I thought, well, holy God.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Somebody get hurt and they got the speeder up there hauling them out or what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I got on up there and I had to. Cop. The strike was on them. And they had a cop out there and he met me. And before I got to camp, I was looking, seeing, boy, the whole woods was lit up. Electric lights. Now there's this little motor running. Yeah. We was in the washroom.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, I asked the cop what was the matter, why was it going on?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, my God, he said, Dick, you won't know what's going on. He said, she's all painted. You got steel bunks. They burn us in the blankets. They burn us in the sugans.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Steel beds and everything. He said, you're lost. So, he took me in the little house and he had a deal bed. Sheets. Blankets. Everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he IWW that done that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Put that on. Well, they had another good rig that I didn't like. They didn't like this Jip one in contract. I like what you're saying. Oh, they didn't like that. Well, now it's all contract. That's where, in one way, the company had to get around them. I could see what was going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the company take advantage of that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, they went to contract them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They get you to work harder?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I don't know if you work much harder. Because most of them old foreman, they'd give you the whiplash and all.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And when I went to Jip one, you see, well, then all of the company all got dinners out to us. And when I went to Jip one, well, now the working man didn't have sense enough himself to take care of it. What he done,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: he worked a noon hour. Jip owners, you see, making more money. Well, the boss and I, we were pretty well acquainted.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He put me on a, I was running the slide and picking up the corners.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, first day we were working out there with the three of us. And we got three car load out and loaded by one o 'clock. So we quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Going down the track and ran out the corner and they said, What's the matter, Johnny? You broke down?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I said, We got three car load out and loaded. What, we got a car apiece.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that enough?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, We quit. And I said, As long as we can get a car, three car apiece, whenever we get them loaded, we're going to camp. Well, he said, You'll hold the price up if you keep that up. And he was a brother's wood superintendent. So he wasn't a bit afraid to tell the truth. Or who would hurt. You know, so. In other words, you got the price on it. That's right. The working man cut his own throat right in the woods. Yeah. Just by not having sense enough to. Well, then I got all the corners skidded out and loaded out and then he gave me a nice, good, big team he had to skid with. And my brother -in -law went swamping for me. There were two of us.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And coming along one time just for a quittin' time, and there I was, sitting on logs, smoking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Coming right up behind me and he said, Hey, Johnny, what's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You broke down. I said, Nope.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he said, What's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And I told him. I said, Bob, we got enough logs today, the two of us. To make our pay. I said, We made about forty dollars a piece today. And I said, That's enough. We're one team. He said, It sure is. And I said, I'm not going above that. I'd rather go below it. Hold the price up.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he said, His brother was wood superintendent. He said, Brother, the time is gonna cut the price on you while it's in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, one of the few days that Tom come up, he come out where I was and he said, What are you gonna do? Keep it a kid log?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And I said, I'll figure on it. Well, he said, There's three skinners here in camp. That's teamsters. I said, I'm not gonna cut the price on you. He said, You're taking better care of your horses than the rest of them. And you three skinners. And he said, Long as you take that good care of your horses, I'm gonna hold the price right up. I listened to him. I said, This is another question. As long as you stay in town. And whenever you quit and leave, when you come back, you'll go to work with the same price as the others. So I found that out. He did. But, his brother, Bob, I was working for him. He said, We can get around and look at that. I did sit down and fix her. So he slipped the price up a little there. So I could make good money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is when you come back to camp?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I was going three, four weeks. You know, that's one thing I like to work working in the woods some days. You work in camp, something happens and you quit. Tell the boss, yep. He go in and he picks you up a hike. And the three of you now, he said, When you go spend that, come back and go to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Fix you up a hike?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, that's what we call getting your pay.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: When I went back to camp, I was at a job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you like to do that? Take off for a couple of weeks or something like that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, yeah. Sometimes. Snow got right deep, I took off there three months. One time, well, I went so late. I was running around a donkey. He's getting me kidneys. We jarred in the shape.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Working on a steam donkey?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, uh, I went in and told him. He said, I'll tell you what you do. You go to Soul Plate. Stay up there for 28 days. And see if it don't help you. So, I did. Well, when he gave me a check. Well, he said, when you go to Soul Plate and come back and feel good, come up and go to work. Well, I didn't get back. I had come back from Soul Plate and I pooped around a couple of weeks and I wrote my brother -in -law. He was lurking and I wrote him a letter. I shouldn't have done it. Of course, he had to tell old Bob about it. He said, in the next day or two, I got a special delivery letter from old Bob to come up and drive to him.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: They don't do that now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? They don't try to get one man?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, they, Well, well, he wanted me to come up and drive a team he had. He had a team that was hard to drive. I didn't think he had a lot of trouble getting their work out of them. I never had a better trouble with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Prince? Was that Prince?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Old Cap of Prince.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was it on, do, Why did the donkey, working on the donkey, jaw your kidneys up?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: What was, Well, I'll tell you, they got them on cables in their passenger stumps. I don't know, there was six or eight lines run out from them to hold them. While they're pulling, you know they're stout. If you get coming around the stump or something, and she hangs up on you, you'll open her up once or twice, see if you can't get her around. Well, when that comes around, that old donkey's pulling her vest, and that comes around loose right quick. Your donkey just goes that way, you know. A boy does a shaky. And that's what hurt my kidneys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were running the donkey then?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. You were standing right on it. Standing right on it, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you been working on it long when it got to your kidneys?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, I'd been working on it, I guess, two months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was so Blake -like? Was that a nice place to go?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, very nice. I went over there. You see, that water, you go there and take your bath. It changes your entire blood in 28 days.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And he had been there, the boys had been there, and that's why he told me to go over. Of course. I went. I'll never regret it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you spend a lot of time in the bath itself?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No. I'll tell you, that water's so strong you take a bath in.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I had room, just about as long as this one, I guess. On the north side, I was all bathtubs. So, as the father right in there all the time looked after him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he come to me and, second day, he said, you never took baths here before, did you not?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, I'm going to tell you something. I'm in here all the time. What he said, if you can't get out of the tub, let me know. So, the second day, you're supposed to take 10 minutes. It just weakened you that fast. And, it didn't use soap. Just rub yourself in that foam, you know.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I guess whenever I put foam in my, he'll clean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How you coming?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, that's fine.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I can quit coughing, I can't quit coughing.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I don't know how I'm going to see Brooks and get something to stop that. I'll tell her, and she's here. She'll be here after a while. I already told her. She said she would see if she couldn't get you an extra digitalis. Right, they better take them to right along now. There's one a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Just one a day. Two. I've been taking two a lot of the time. You didn't know nothing about. Oh yeah, well, you can't do that all the time though. I know, they're poison.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: These towels are strong. You get those and I'll call them at the drug store so they'll be filled. It's poison, yeah. You might take an ocean to come home any time. I can't get too much of it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, you said this guy was going to, told you to let him know if you couldn't get out of the van. You were in there for ten minutes the second day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you need him?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, I made it. But I had to roll over and get on my hands and knees to get out.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: It's just that strong. It's just that strong.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: God, you know, I went in there and wondered, you know, they had two tables in there, I don't know how long it was. They must have been forty -five feet. They were full the whole time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jesus said, what's in there the second morning?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Jesus told her, you know, you'd be eating away and drinking. Drop everything you need. That's a toilet. The fourth day I left mine too. That's a toilet. Oh, she does clean you up. Mmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you come back pretty much as good as new then?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, yeah. Felt good. Limbers are kept. Slept well over nights. Didn't have to get up before one of my kidneys was bothering me. I'd have to get up sometimes ten, twelve times over nights. That weakened the weather.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't go back on a donkey after that, did you? You didn't go back on the donkey then?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, I went back. You may have cut out one piece too much, I don't know. Yeah, I went back and finished the job. It didn't take but a little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did anybody put it back in here?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You know, this is talking about Jipo and what you were saying. You said the IWW, they was against Jipo. They was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you against it too?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I didn't care for it. I knowed we didn't Jipo a week till I could see what was going to happen. But I'm saying it. The lumberjack is going to cut his own throat. Work a noon hour.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, I do have that. Well, before, you know, the company all sprung us out hot lunch at noon. It is extra. You don't need it at all. Well, then they all ordered when I got Jipo and they all ordered lunch buckets. Well, I think we've got to have a carload of this. Lunch buckets. Now give them all one. You have two if you're on one of these hands.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: About two weeks. The cook said there's no use in making lunches anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the hot lunch better?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh boy. Yeah, I brought it out there, you know, covered up, wrapped up. Smoking hot. Always. Raining or something, it tastes awful good.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Last one time I had one feller, you know. I hadn't took the hot lunch away from them. It was about a few days and it was raining. They're sitting out there under a tree eating. This one feller, they're grouching about it. He liked the hot lunch. I'm going to show you. Another feller was sitting there by him eating. He said, you son of a bitch, I hope you starve to death on it. He said he wanted it, he got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what are you going to do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Eat her, God damn you!

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You know,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: them old fellers, they'd come straight out with it. They didn't beat around the bush.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I like to work in the woods, God bless us a lot.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You're saying though that Deformin kind of drove the men out of the time. Yeah, used to. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did that work?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I mean, I was thinking men wouldn't care for that very much. Well, it was all used to it from the early days. You know, them old formans, you know, they used to. We had one over here on the seal gank. Lots of times they had the logging camp and then they'd bring a seal gank to Yemen. My coiled run the seal gank. Now he'd go every morning or at noon. Kick the door open and roll up, roll out. Well, that was it. He'd either roll his seconds or get ready to travel or go to work. That way at morning and that way at noon. So, a man was used to it. They never thought nothing about it. They changed over the IWW, took it over and they kind of changed things. But, then, you know, they... Gippo then, he kind of taken over and he... Get up to my table, eat me his last hot cake. He'd eat it before he got to the barn to get his team. So he could get to work.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No wonder they're here.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: These guys, it sounds like some of these guys, like... TP Jones' brother, Bob Jones. It sounds like he was a little more interested. He was lenient with the crew. But most of these guys would try to get everything out of you they could. That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that do anything for them? Did that give them more money, make them look better? Why were they pushing the men like that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, that I don't know. But, you take every camp, I worked on several of them. All the foremen, they got their men. They got the pets. And they favor them. Well, all you can do is let her go, I guess. Or that's the way they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the foremen's pets be their personal friends?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, yeah. Bad. Yeah.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I had one in Tom Kelly. I worked for him. I worked there in five. I worked in the kitchen there for 18 months. I got tired of that. I never did like it. So then, I come down on... I took our little wood doll out down to Princeton and Cordwood. We'd cut that winter and so I... I'm getting that out and Jeezy sent me a letter to come up. Had a new job for me. He knowed I didn't like it. So I went up and he put me to scaling. Out in the woods, that was good. That wasn't hard work. Easy.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, there's nothing to do with that. But, Jesus, he would put a lot. One winter there, he was down in camp six and I was scaling down there. 17 saws. Scale after. That makes lots of logs. In good timber that is. Don't make as many logs as that's about scale.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. He'd talk the work right on to you. Don't think he wouldn't. Well, I'll tell you what he was. He was an old, right old cook. He put... cooking on the line when they put the inland through to Moscow and folk there. Well, he cooked on that. And the fellas that was cooking there then had plunkied for him down here in Blues. When they was making that cut there.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Shorty Tribble, his old brother, was cooking. We were working in the kitchen with him and Tom learned to cook.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: That old Tom was a good cook.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's where Shorty Tribble learned to cook? Tom Kelly?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Tom Kelly, yeah. Tommy left there along one path where he was working there. Shorty could not make any bread that was worth a darn to eat. So, he tried it for four or five days and every time Kelly had a certain place to eat, they all had their certain place to eat at the table. Every time I'd go by with Tommy again and he'd say, he'd point at the bread. So, one morning he'd come in there and he said, what's the matter, he ought to go to cook Bluser.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he say?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Called him Bluser.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Bluser? He said, what's the matter Bluser, what's the matter with your bread?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Shorty said, I don't know, I've done every damn thing I can think to do to it and I can't make it come out of it. Well,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Tom said, I'll tell you what to do. I'll go turn the crew on out, the rest of them, and see if they're all working and I'll come back. Wash them. Put on a clean shirt and show you how. Oh, he did, he's back in about half an hour, an hour. He worked the bread down and he showed up on the bread now, he said, let that go. Don't touch it. That bread, he said, don't work it down. Said, you've been working it down two or three times, I don't pay on this. So, Shorty did. Geez, we've had good bread for supper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He come in and looked at me and winked and he said, hey Bluser, how's your bread come out?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, Shorty said, it's alright I guess. He wasn't no damn well Tom, we're trying to get a raise out of it. He seen that wasn't going to happen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He wasn't letting it rise, was that the deal? He wasn't letting it rise?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, yeah. And I tell you what happened, is that long, oh, right after first year and his new bread, new wheat.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And Tom said that new wheat, you couldn't work it like you did the old wheat. If you did, it wouldn't rise up. Well, I don't know. That was that little shorty just done like Tom did, and then on him mixed her up, worked her down, threw her up on the bread rack and let her go to.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: She started running over the edge of the pan and they just took her down, rolled her out, put her in the pan and let her raise again and beg her. He had good bread.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So I learned something too. I heard that the men were pretty particular about the food. Oh! You take a bunch of old lumberjacks, it's hard to lay out, look where it is.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You know, one time we were there, they didn't have, oh, we had, just after the strike and they were kind of settling down, the other boy was coming back in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, they talked to me once, I was there one day and I said, well, today was the last day, what's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, we said we're going down tomorrow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I said we can get 10 cents an hour, tomorrow, at Blackwell and Rutledge. Well, of course, there's a regular old time bunch of jacks. I didn't pay no attention to that. But I noticed something and I couldn't figure it out.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, they could draw out all their money and went in, spoke and blowed in and come back to, I think they come back to Rutledge's, there at Fernwood. Well, I worked there one payday. They come, there they come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long have those roses been blown off out there? What's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I thought you fellas were going over there and making big money. Well, said the money was alright, they got a damn check, we couldn't eat her.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Very funny. Of course, I could all blame it on to the Chuck.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, I could all blame it on to the Chuck. He was a good cook, shorty was a good cook. He only cooked everything good and done and good flavor, good taste to it. And the best meat -cutter I ever saw. Well, he had a bunch of stuff of his own. And he could get you a good steak right up around the horn. Oh, I never seen a man could cook meat like you could and save it, save on it, see.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You were talking about being a teamster and with this team of horses that was a tough one to handle and you were telling me about that when I was here last time. But I couldn't figure why it was a tough one to handle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why you could handle them when these other when these other teamsters like this good one couldn't do it? Why was it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You figured out that... Well, it's the way I treated them.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You know, a horse is like a human being. You got to treat him good if you get anything out of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what happened to this feller?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He tried to whip -break him and couldn't. So, I went to put the harness on him that morning and boy, I went behind him and he kicked at me. Both feet! Well, I said, that won't work. I'll try it the other way. Well, I got... I had a place built up between the team, between the horses. And I got over next to the bay and went up the front end and he struck at me. Well, I said, that won't work.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I had the blacksmith and the barn boss and the wool cook. We couldn't get the harness on him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, what are you going to do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, I'm going to go get some sugar. So, I went and got a cup of sugar. Give him a bite or two, you know. Mmm! That was good. Boy, they got right there where we got in France.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I didn't know then what had happened to him. I know there's something. Well, I just couldn't figure it out what had they done to him that made him that bad. I had... Next to the second, third day, the other team, he drug out a log and, oh, there's a limb on it, 8, 10 feet long. Laying in the road and I didn't want to get the old fella tangled up in it. Get him to kicking at me out there, so... I walked around in front of him and picked it up. When I'm picked up here, he come with mouth open and a paw on the air. I said, oh, I know what's another thing. I went, ow!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I tried to whip break you, didn't I?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And the breaking went the other way.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, so... We got along pretty good from then on. This deal that... Tell me about this deal that De Foreman made. He said he'd pay you and then you also could get... Yeah, I got Dave's wages out of it, besides. And you got wages for... you got money for the scale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You got gypo for the scale, is that right?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No. Yeah, I got the scale out of it. But then I got Dave's labor for driving the team. For driving him. Did you get the whole...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: how much of the scale did you get?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, just whatever we get, Dave. It was a good team. And I was careful about breaking them. Dave got Dave killed every time you asked him. So that's what helps, you know. So... I come out all right. An old foreman, Tracy, Art Tracy, he come around in a day or two and I was... working the old fella and he said, you know an awful job, man. I said, no, old job too. He said, you too.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, if you think you can handle him, go ahead. Just pick you up a little switch and go... pick up the lines and see how you come out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know why he said I won't do that, but I said, what are you hollering about?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I still think you better job me. All right. That's your brief. Go ahead. But I did die. Worked there. Oh, I worked there, I guess, about two months and a half.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So an old noggily come down one morning, one day, and he come out in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's standing there just a good way and he said, Dick, he said, don't you think that we've pulled old Tracy's legs long enough?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, you're a run -nut. Well, he said, I'm gonna give you back your old thing. And he said, I'll give you a beer with that thing. Pretty good, nice, easy skinner. So Noggle asked me why the marathon horse and I just up and told him. He took me away from me. And when Joe left there, Joe was there about 18 months. And when he left, Noggle gave him a team. That's the last old captain ranch in the woods. He took them home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Noggle just kept, I said, when in the name of God did you do that for?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he said, if somebody else get him, I'll beat him. He's too good a horse for that. And he said, I know Joe won't. So Joe didn't joke ever until he died. On old wood one day, and the old fella just started up the hill. He got about halfway up it. And Joe, he wanted to give him a rest. All the old fella just fell over dead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: His name was Captain Prince?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Boy, they braided that black horse. Had an awful good dinner on Beluga's. He brung them down well with. He earned harness to bay, but I knowed him. And old team was coming that day. He said, Dick, I brung a team down for you to work. And I said, old Captain Prince. He said, Prince still got harness on him. Boy, I said, I thought you was a good skinner. Well, he said, not for that one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, well, what's the matter with you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Red Lucas is his name. Well, he said, I don't know. I don't want an undoing. All right. Well, I said, we'll wait until after supper and I'll go up and pull the harness off of him. I went up, walked in and called him by name. And he just looked right straight at me. Right, laid his head right back in his rib and looked at me. Right in the face of both eyes. I got a little closer and he come in to hunt and sugar. Just knotted me onto the arms and all over. Well, I didn't have any, but soon as I got the harness off of me. Got him. Cradled him and I went down to the cuckoos and told someone to give him. Thought the old fella hadn't forgotten neither had I.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you like working horses better than other kinds of working?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, yeah. See, I was raised on a farm and I liked horses better than I did cats. Of course, now I could have had, well, the company got the cats in 1918. Got him in there, came six hours more than I was. I took the show on logs and they brung that cat out where we were working. And we had no, they had no rock rails on them. You couldn't keep them on the track. On a little stick, one sided and she'd run over the track and oh boy. That's on another stick, on a hitch. Back it up, run it out and let it out and get you a pole. You could lay under it and run that one side so we could climb back on again.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: They said, right at the rig, that's the old oak. You know, coming home and getting a chance to stay at home would be real. Well, you know. Good change. Down below here now are the folks that live down there. Well, two miles and a quarter right down the creek from here. Well, I was long before I was married. When I come down here, I stayed at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Saved that dollar and, what was we paying then?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I believe we were paying a dollar and twenty cents a day for it. Saved that.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You know.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: But, the fella staying at home, he's not with the crew.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: There's lots of things he can do with his team up there in the evening. Dress them up, carry them up.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Gonna lick them up. Take good care of them, you know. So, I had one team there. I had a team I drove about five years, I guess, a little better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the old nugget would come out one day and he said, He said, you got any idea how many logs that you skidded with that team?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he said, you skidded enough logs of that team to Corduroy a road to Seattle and halfway back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, Jesus Christ, you done a lot of figuring didn't you know?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I didn't do that. I said, black dead. Well, he took, I think, four hundred logs and figured up the width of each log. Of course, it was right on there. He could get it right off the scale.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He just figured it up. God, he was good and figured it out. I wish that was as good as he was. He'd be talking about something business, figuring, logging something. He'd stand there and listen and he'd have it all figured up and he got done talking. You know, a fella like that pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do there? What was his job there?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Clerking. You know, you done all the figuring for the camp.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: But he got told that one boy I never got to take her to my life.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: We was all breaking our decking lines. It was all old. And Kelly said, Dick, I'm going into camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are you going into camp for?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I broke my decking line twice while I was there. They brought her up and went on. I said, I'm going to get you fellas. I'm going to get you some new decking lines. He said, I'm getting tired the way you're breaking them. All this and that. And he said, we're going to stop it. So we said, I believe we'll go in right now and have back that where the clerk has us, whether we're good figures. Well, there's only one more day till payday in the month.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So Kelly told him, he said, Black, there's another one through town. We didn't have no phone out there. The phone up the bow hill in order. Barrel of chain. Decking line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Barreling chain? What kind of chain?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Decking line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, barrel of chain, did you say?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: A barrel of chain. A barrel of chain. It come in barrels. Oh, the barrels were there, I don't know. I guess they had a whole 50, 60 gallon. That was full of chain. They was our downer. So Tom went in the office, he told me, and he said, Black, I want you to go to town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I want you to go down and order a barrel of chain and stay down there till a van comes down out of the bowl coat. And I'll have him load it. Get it up here tonight. And the old Black said, I can't do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tom said, why?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he said, I got my payroll made out.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Tom said, I'll tell you what I'll do. I'll make a deal with him. He said, you go order the chain and I'll pay for it. And the next month, if we have to get another barrel, you can pay for it. So without shooting, they're pretty straight at him. So he got the barrel of chain down there.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, he sure got it down there. But Tom was awful good for rigging.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he'd have another talk in one time as we were down at the office chewing rags with him and raining. And we couldn't go out and he said, something about rigging I don't know. And I said, Tom, you're the best foreman I ever worked for for rigging. All I said was, I'll tell you, a van ain't logging unless he got it. He said, it takes good rigging to log with. Well, he was right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he say?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He was good with the rigging. He kept good rigging for you. He kept the stuff in good repair. You'll bet you. He had a backsmith, but he didn't keep that rigging up, he didn't tell you.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I had one backsmith and he was here with him most of the time. Bill Seating. Boy, he was a good backsmith. He built anything. Anything he wanted to build, he built it right there in the blacksmith's forge. It used to be rainy days, I'd help him. Just help him to kill time. Boy, that old fella couldn't work anything out and wear it down. Figure it out, you know, and she'd work.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He'd quite a hand to drink and I... Of course, I was staying over at home and I had an old Dodge car and I met the bridge coming home one night. I was a little full, all right. So, I supposed to come and get him next morning and take him down so he gets home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he come out the barn and he said, where the hell is your car?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, I'm going home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, what's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, I missed the bridge last night, they already covered it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, I broke every leaf in one front spring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why didn't you bring it over?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I don't know. He said, you go get it and I'll finish taking care of your team. And run right in the shop. And we'll tear up and weld them together. My God, he welded all them springs and I'd do it three years after that and that's still a whole...

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, he knows what he's doing when he come to welding. So, one time that we didn't have no welding compound and one of my horses built a shoe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We were talking to him the night before, he said, can you put it on? I said, what's the matter Bill?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I got no welding compound to throw court.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Boy, I said, what am I going to do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, come up in the morning and we'll straighten it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I went up in the morning and he said, why can't you build some welding compound? I said, what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I'll tell you what to do. He said, you go down to the crook and get you some nice clean sand and moist it. Just small stuff. Bring it up. Chop it up right fine. And he said, when you get that done, go get your pint bottle glass. Break it up, chop it up fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, he said, when you get that done, why mix the two of them together?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, I thought, I'll learn something. So, he said, you know, we shot the horse and he put the tow course on. Well, the tow course felt just as good as it would if he had welding compound on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The tow?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Tow court.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, he told me, he said, Dick, you know, that is my first job in Wisconsin. He said, we is 80 mile in town up on the river. And he said, that was my job. He said it on a 15, 16. That was my job getting sand and bottles and making compounds for the blacksmith. And he said I would help him in here and there. I said, I got a long war to last. He said, I am doing pretty good helping him and all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, he told Bill one day, he said, you coming back next year?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. Alright, he said, if you come back, he said, you can have his job, he is not coming back. I said, that will take you up one notch. So, Bill, he went back up. He is a helper, blacksmith helper. And he said he was a good smith. He said I helped him two years there and then he said I took over.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Blacksmithing. And he said he is a better blacksmith than I was. And I said I do not see how. Well, he said he was.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, he learned all Bill and all he knows. Of course, now Bill picked up a lot of it himself. And he is good, very good at figuring out anything. You go in and tell him what you wanted. He is one blacksmith that I seen.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You be working. You come out in the woods and see how your rigging was. How your pea region, how your canticle, tongs, how everything was working for you. And if you had something that did not look just right. He said, hey, do not you suppose you better bring that in tonight. We will work it over. Well, that is working all right, Bill. No, it is not good enough. So, I will tell you, if he made you a canticle, you showed the log. If you did not want to catch the log, you better move back because you would have it before you knowed it. That old hook would never let go.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, that is something. Sounds like he really looked after his work. He did. Well, I went sending up out there in the woods. Bill come out and he picked up the canticle that I was using.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, where did you get that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, it is here in Arkham. I said, that is what the other fella was using yesterday.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, an auger, he come along about that time and he said, say, Claire. He said, you have got any so and so iron up there, yup. He said, you go up and send me down. I think you want 40 feet of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Claire said, what in the hell are you going to do now?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I am going to make these boys some canticle of my own. So, he made them. So long and everything. So, you know, he knowed what he was doing. So, I got a hook out there yet that he made me. He made me two.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I used one of them all the time I was sending up. Careful with it. Never sprung it or nothing.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, we got down. He said, I was staying at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, you know where that old windwall redwood tree is?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yup. He said, you go home tonight. You know what I was going to do. I said, go on the lower side of that and look. There is some brush and stuff out in there. He said, you dig under that brush and there is a brand new canticle under there. That I built. He said, I want you to take it home. Remember me, boy. I got it out of there yet. Of course, Peggy caught a log, she didn't even catch it. But she did. And boy, I showed her and I'm going to spit the handle out of that canticle. Or the top end. He hid that. He wasn't supposed to give it to you so he hid it in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He hid it so I could find her to take it home with me. Yeah. That's nice. Sounds like a nice man. Yup. He always went overboard for me. He made me a pair of skid and tongs.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: They were made over two years and I never used them. So this fella there, he was breaking a pair of tongs about every day. I just happened to be in the shop.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I see them new tongs laying on the floor.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So he said, Amy, let me tell you something. He said Dick has used them pair of tongs he got right now for a little over two years right now. He never breaks them.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I have an idea, you better hang them new tongs back up so they don't belong to you. I said, I've got them for him every two years ago and I'm ready for him. He said he'd take care of his rigging. Not the fellas I'd take care of first. They're less trouble to me. So Amy went to them, hung them back up and never said a word. About all he could do is go to the old mill would have made him. I wonder why that is. Some guy like him would keep breaking it. He didn't understand the woods. He might take a tong and throw it right around the stump and leave the crook in there and try to roll his log out and pull him straight against the stump. You can't do that without breaking something directly.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Boy, I worked. He was top -loading. They wanted to know why he wouldn't send up. And the fella that was sending up, Johnny, Powderpuff Johnny, he's a good hook man. He was sending up but he worked at it one day and Amy was on top.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: That night Johnny said, well I think I'll go down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Boy said, what's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, what do you think?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, you think I'm going to work out there and him sending up. Me sending up for a man like that on top but never worked on top before.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Boy said, you'll have to get somebody else. Old Knobel was coming in the door but about that time he just come down below us. So him and Jack talked a little bit. The fella said, come on back where I was scurrying the team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Nogga said, do you suppose you can send up?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, I can try it. He said, I know you have. He said, you might be a little out of practice. He said, he'll come back to you so go ahead. So I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I worked with him one day and I went in that night and I said, is that all you got for a man on top?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well I said, count me out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Next morning we went out and he said, who are you going to get?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, I'm going to get my partner Andy Kidwell. You want to weigh about 120 pounds but boy have you ever got a log of every of them skids that is his. He took them out. I never let his chain get away from him.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Old Nogga would come up at Nogga and Andy and I would have been working there about an hour I guess that morning. Nogga looked over and he said, well that little fella I didn't think he could work on top. I said, you don't know him like I do.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, he said, alright. Well Andy and I worked together then two years before. So we said, well let's make it up to Andy some way. I said, that's up to you. So we finished the job.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You know, you said to me before that Laird would come out and he would check the men on the job too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would he do that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No. No. I never seen Laird in the woods once. That was the last time I ever seen him. I don't know whether he's up there. Old Camp Five and I was working in the kitchen. We got our work done after dinner. We went out in the marin with a load of logs. Oh, I'd say probably 150 feet above camp. It was raining a little over there. Four or five of them was up there and they had umbrellas. Bill Bailey, he was running the marin and he was good with it. So we was getting a few long logs. So they kept crowding in, crowding in. Bill looked at me and winked. I know something's going to happen but I couldn't think what. Old Eddie was standing there. Oh boy, he was so interested, you know. Rainin' and holdin' his umbrella in on. Old Billy swung the log around, grinning and looked at me. He just winked and he just let her down on the umbrella easy. Easy down, easy down. Old Eddie didn't know what was happening. He put his hand on it, slipping in his hands. He tried to get it up and he couldn't. He looked up and seen that log. Boy, he left the umbrella in the hole.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: But... He took the hint. He knew old Bill was just asking for room.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Boy, he shouldn't have been in there.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He assumed somebody else was in there. I suppose he thought, well, they'll get killed and we'll have to pay for them.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Some of them fellas, you know. They get awful wise. They get a lot of ideas. Still, they don't know nothing. Jim O 'Connell down there, I was... ...loading trucks and they just... Had the long trucks in there, you know. Once in a while they'd break her cable. Loading cable. Well, whenever they broke one, had that trailer about... ...up where she belonged, she'd go down that ramp and... ...her tongue would snap a couple of times and when she straightened out she'd just break her and go right on. Jim and this other fella from Billings, from Lewiston. They was down there watching them, working them. They was following that trailer right up.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, I stopped the trucker and I said, fellas, I don't believe I'd follow that trailer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, if he breaks that loading cable... ...a trailer will go back there, surpass it, it'll catch you both. You didn't have a chance to get out of the road. Boy, their faces turned white and they got out of the road and stayed out. Didn't have to tell them a second time.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You know,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I was going to ask you too about what you thought of Potlatch, the town, when it was Company Town there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know you didn't live there much, did you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I wouldn't live there at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the place, Company Town?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, it's Company Town, a one man town.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And that don't work. A one man town and all that. You've been raised out in the country, that don't suit you.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, they used to always try to get me to move to town. I was working down in Tamale on the pond, unloading low. They wanted me to go down there and live. I couldn't see it. I'm having a hard enough time getting along up here.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, they've never got over it. The town has never got over it. No, here this spring, you know, both ministers, well, one of them retired and the other one quit from the losing church.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And so, yeah, they're going to have one minister for the little churches. Well, I said, whoop, that ain't going to work either. There's been one man town too long for that. They got all the old heads in there and that's just what happened. And it didn't work. They rode up a time or two and had two or three ministers in there. Well, when they're Presbyterians, they finally give up. They went on an animal hunt, tried to give old churches a little saying.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: But it's getting along a little better every time. But it hurts them. I don't quite understand what that means.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, if you've got a one man town, that means that people can't work together?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No. One man's boss in the whole thing.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: It's just one man boss and that don't work.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: It doesn't work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they used to have on Nob Hill?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: All the brass used to live up there. Yeah, that's on the south side. That's on that hill just as you come into town. Kind of like there was two classes in the town. One up on Nob Hill and the other one's down. Yeah, that's it. Well, you see all the ones that had the good job, they were on Nob Hill. Of course, the other fellas, they weren't just a common man, common laborer. They weren't rainy man up there either. They helped out for them. Well, that made it bad.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, now they incorporated the town. All these fellas had bought the property. I don't know what's the matter with them. I would say, ready to get up and speak your piece or say anything or what. But I know if I had, I'd get up and speak my piece.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it a lot harder to get union and organize in the sawmill and potlatch than it was out in the woods?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't no harder?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Wasn't as hard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you didn't favor it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, I wasn't. Neither was the company. So it hung fire for about, oh, I'm sorry. So they sent a carload of lumber from Livingston to Chicago. And I woke up one morning and I was just sitting down here on the track and I was just... Well, I got up back there and they went and unloaded.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why wasn't they unloaded? Wasn't union loaded?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, we all joined the union. Yeah, I bet. Boy, they sent four or five of them out with a meal all around, signing us up.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I guess you couldn't blame them. They'd either tie them up or they were going to have to shut down and get another crew.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I don't know.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: The unions may be all right, but I don't go for them.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I was thinking about Princeton and the town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much to do with the town when you lived out here in the early days? Was there much going at the town then?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. Well, we lived there all six weeks when I first come here. It would be about a place.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Then after that, they kept buying a little property there in Princeton and then worked at the mills. They'd drive back and forth. They didn't want to live down there in town, so that's the way they worked it. They'd buy them a little piece of property here and rent them and drive back and forth.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: They used to have anything on the 4th of July in town. Oh, boy, did they. Oh, you, Bicky. They used to celebrate. And the fella over here in the flat, Burke Rowell, he always scraped around and got a little pair in there before. Had a pair. He'd sell it and sell that and get money for small prizes, you know, for pumpkins and everything. Well, he sold out and moved away. Well, when he done that, well, that fella.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, well, she had to change.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yeah?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yep, everything changes here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you use to trade, do most of your trading at Princeton?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I was at Washington down there, E -Runner store for several years there. I was off. I've been in there for two years. And right after the depression, I was in the company.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And I went to work on the county, where the county is on the road, Kyle King and I.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: God, I owed him nearly $500 every time I went in. Tell him, well, if I can get a deal, I ain't got my money. I know it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you want? Tell him, now, you sure that's all?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, a fella like that, nice, he said, I know you'll get your money sometime. So when I got my money, I went in and paid him off. And I appreciated it, because we got all the living reasons. I had five kids at that time. Come with a hand, I thought I'd get all these groceries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he known you for a long time before that? Huh? Had he known you for a long time before that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: His father had. He runs the store there first. His father had run the store there.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I kind of think the old fella was imprecated in the store then, too.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: But nobody would never know that he wasn't or he wasn't. I don't know how he hung around the store lots. He hung around the store lots.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think everybody that needed credit got it then around Princeton, or was there some people that couldn't get it, like you could?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: There's some that couldn't get it. You know, if you don't tell him, the poor devil don't keep his credit up or he don't. He hasn't got much directly. Okay.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, I know that, well, when I first got married, I had fourteen daughters left. And I bought a fella out there. I bought all his, what stock he had in his house, his lease on the place for a year. Well, I went down to Potlach to get the money on it. I told him, I said, I'll go to Potlach and get the money. He used to go to the Fish Cross one time and wouldn't let me have it. I'd done business with him for a year and that's a dollar.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, old Lemons there in Princeton, he used to let lots of money out, you know. I'd borrowed money off of him three or four times. So, I stopped seeing him. No, he said, I haven't got a dick but to tell you what to do. If you come down tomorrow afternoon, bring your car, I'll tell you where you can get it. No, see, you do get it. Well, he took down the old National Bank there in Bloose. So, we went at him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He gave an introduction to him and we talked there for a little bit and pretty soon Max said, What would your mom, what's her business?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, I told him, I'd like to get a, need $150 for a bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, what are you going to do with it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I explained it. First time I was ever in the bank. But, of course, old Lemons was with him there where he done all his banking business.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, yeah, he said, I just got it on a straight note. Being the old man Lemons was with me, that's it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, Lemons told him, he said, I know he's good because he said he borrowed money off of me several times. He's always paid it back. Always paid it a little before due somehow.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, McPherson, he said, we'll let that go. He said, I'll give you the money, don't worry. So, I got the money and come on home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened with people like them that couldn't get money if they needed it, say, during the Depression? Or couldn't buy food, couldn't buy what they needed at Wasson, what would they do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I don't know, they didn't get back. Got by, I think, a lot of it on commodities and stuff. Also, time put out.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: We didn't get too many of them. No, we didn't get, well, we didn't need them all the time. I'd rather let somebody have them, but I know didn't need them.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, some of them got down pretty tough.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, some of them got pretty tough.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: All together.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He wouldn't kill a hog or a beef or anything unless I was right. And they get that, that's he didn't kill the hogs. Oh, that doesn't do it all right, he said. You're waiting on thinking of the damn bleeds. Oh, that was a little rough, anyhow. So, he got a great big hog. Oh, she was, I think, about, I don't know, 700. We skinned her. Well, he kept her for ten days until the sign got right. No, he said, you butters, this is how we're having this. He said, I'll have mom to cook some of that side pork. Today noon first. And he said, you see if there's any difference in it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, she fried it, done a good job. So, sitting on the table, all the grease was out of it. Just fried nice, but still just as thick as it was when she cut it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, we got about half down to Aiken and he said, see any difference in the meat. Well, he said, it's nice, old as cake, good. That's why I waited today to kill it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: he planted everything. Well, I know there in Nebraska we had a three corner fence come in there on the line like that. And Dad planted his little three corner strip and spuds. The other other did.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, that's fine. So, boy, all the butters vines got up high that day. Bloomed and boy, that's a nice looking fudge you ever saw.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, we done that one day hoeing and he was out hoeing. He said, yeah Dave, he said, what'd I tell you. Once you figure these fudges don't be any good, Dad said they're alright if they don't all go to vines.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: That little old vine is about that high, you know, but they're blooming and going to beat the devil. And we got a little double the amount of spuds that he got. And his vines is up more that high. Now ours are about like that. So, we got, well, I asked him what is that. Well, he said, you planted yours in the wrong time, then we went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what difference does that make?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, mine is growing under the ground and yours is growing on top.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how he figured when he ought to plant? What made him, what was guiding him there?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He went by the almanac. It's all marked out in that, you know. I never could read it. I asked him one time how he judges the forts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Judges the what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: How he knew when to look at it. And he said, well, when we go on the moon, the moon. He said, if the moon is new and growing, it's alright. But if you wait until it's just almost full, and then butcher. But if you wait until after it's full and starts on the decline, then it will all fry out to green. Huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We haven't always followed that, so why don't you hurry?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I'll tell you, it's got something to do with it. I don't know why.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: But, the moon even controlled the tide of the ocean.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Now, how it gets out, I don't know. But I know it does.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have your tape on?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, go ahead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are you going to say?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: We raised rats for a while. You know. And, well, for experimental purposes, the university, you know, sold rats. We had over 3 ,000 at a time, you know. And we noticed, according to the moon, we kept records. And according to the moon, which sounds ridiculous, I have no answers. According to the moon, the conception was almost three times as good during the growing part of the moon as it was in the reclining part of the moon. We would only have to breed about a third as many rats on the... Don't ask me why, I don't know. But it's very true, and the litters were bigger than they were on the other. Now, science will go fly a kite. But it proved out over a period of years that this was very true. Yeah, it's a really interesting... I don't doubt it. This is just according to our records.
