Certainly nothing official, but we did keep... He had mentioned it to the kids at the university, and he says, well, this is where you start keeping records. This is where you prove a theory, where you have the equipment to do it with. And so we started keeping records, and that did prove out.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I haven't moved yet. I don't know.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I know a dude down there at Johnny's one day. He said, he wanted to go climbing.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, not today, not tomorrow, not the next day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said the climb was going to come in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he said the moon brings them in. So we waited three days and went down there, and boy, we just a little late that morning. Boy, all the sand was just bubbling this way and all.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did Indians get by?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: They went by the moon. They could tell you what the winter was going to be, what the summer was going to be. All the seasons, they know what they was. They had no books, no nothing.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: There was one old, fuller Indian there. I was working on a bird to saw me on. I had four Indians working for me. And three of their dads, their father, come up and lived with them. And they was folk and Indians. As a matter of fact, I got a lot of my advice. It was off of him. He was 96.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Still pegging around in a brick of jape. And he told me, you know, he said, Dick, he said, we got enough of flogging.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How's that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he said they may just sign up. I said, I'm a folk and Indian. He said they may just sign up. And give us a reservation around folk and Indians. Well, he said we had it two years and they took it away from us. We didn't have no fish, no fish division or nothing. Well, he sent us over to Coeur d 'Alene. He said it was over there two years. And he said that was no good. First, then he said they sent us down to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Lapwe? This first, Lapwe?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, that place between here and Fokan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yakima?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No. It's right on the 95 between here and... No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't belong to the gourdland reservation, see? Plumber?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, see, he... Well, it don't matter. Plumber. But what's the name of the... I think what they call it, it's a mission. See, they got a mission there. Yeah.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, the way she went...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do we have seven buttons maybe or six?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No. We're just going to see how it's doing.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I think those are four and a half of them. It'll have to be him that's the boss. So, he wasn't happy about it, though. Oh, boy, he burned him up. When they took the... Fokan, when they took the Indians' reservation there. The whites. Moved them over to Gourdland. Burned him up and from then on he... Upset and backwards. Nice old brother. He wasn't nasty about it, but he just did the plain juice. How the whites treated him. It was pretty bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, they blamed the Indians for fighting. What we do is the Russians come in there and... Try to overhaul us like we did them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We all going to fight back?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Right now. That's the reason I don't go for that gun law. They could, they're liable to. Okay. The Russians are not too dependable. You can be safe talking to me. I'm in for you. I'm not sure when you can make your life home even. That's their business, I guess, not mine.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You know, Dick, I was going to ask you something about the difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you raise your kids about the same way your parents raised you? Or was it much different?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, there was very little difference. I made a mind at home. I told them when they got away, Mom wanted them to do the same way. If they ever got into anything or done anything that wasn't right, we'll let Dad pay for it one way or the other. So... Made our final dandy.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Boy, he got arrested once. Boy, I laughed like right over that.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He... Trying to go with the cop's daughter down in Pluce. Well, I know the other feller and he's pretty grouchy and... Johnny aggravated the old feller then with his car down in Pluce one night. Well, I kind of figured there's something going on. Went on, went on, we sat and ate breakfast. See, boy, there was something bothering him. We was working on the pot of that sawmill. I bought him a good car and was using it to go back and forth.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I could see in his face, we got about half done eating and he said, how much money you got in the back, Dad?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I had no dinner, they picked him up. So we told him. I told him. Well, he said, I'll give a check for you on your name. This is J .H. Benz and mine is J .L. Benz. So we just wrote it to J .L. He told me what he done. I said, that's alright. We give him a check for his plane and come back home that night.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: What he done. Went to Pluce and crossed that aerial highway and... made his U -turn there. You can't do that. And he said, Dad, I know what I did and shouldn't have done it, but I did.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And I said, well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, well, how you gonna do about it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he said, you gotta go back Tuesday night. We're gonna have a hearing. I said, alright, I'll go with you. So we went down and we went down across the aerial highway. No aerial highway sign up. I don't know, we come back up the other side, wasn't none over there. Now, I said, let me tell you something. You ask them why they haven't got their signs up. It takes two signs, one on that corner and one over here. And I said, they're just using that now to pick up people that don't know them. So we went in. So... He said, they asked him why he done it. Well, he said, I didn't see no sign up cause of the aerial highway. Said, you're supposed to have a sign up there. Said, I was a stranger and old. He said, I shoulda known. I just thought it would just slip my mind. Well, they talked around there a while and finally gave him back twelve dollars. Took him loose. Now, he said, I'm coming down tomorrow night and a few fellas ain't got them aerial signs up. Two of them, one over here and one over there. Said, I'm gonna see what I can do with the city.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So... We went down the next night and they was up. One over there and one over here.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Now they tore them down to its purpose. To catch people. So they... Well, you know, that's the way the cops are in Bruce. That's the way he gets his money. Most of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This guy, your son was going with his daughter, huh?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He was trying to. Old brother didn't like him and he just aggravated the old brother. Of course, that was alright, I guess. Yeah. You know, you cross the railroad track up to the end of the blues. Johnny turned in one time and come down the railroad track on the rail with his car down there. And... Sam... Hah! Rode off. Cop caught him at that. Well, that didn't help.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I didn't know a car would stick to a set of rails like they do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ever see that tried out?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, they'll stick right on them. Yeah. You just let your front steering wheel go. You'll stay right on the rail.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, them kids, they learned lots of stuff that us older people didn't know. Yeah.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Did you make some more coffee, Peggy, or is what's in the pot all there is? So you never really wanted to farm very much yourself, or did you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I made some more, but it's cool. Johnny, that's the boy. He worked over there for... Hugo Walter. He worked over there three years. He was his kid and Hugo, he wanted to lease him his old place and find his own machine. He wouldn't do it. Better plug in your eatery. There won't be no gold in there, honey. I was running camp at the time. It'll make your hands hurt worse. If you go over to him, so he'd come up. Well, he's working at the meal too, part time.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He'd come up and ask him what I thought about, and I said, I said, you're 18, a little over, you're old enough, you ought to know what you want. I don't. You suit yourself. So he said, I'll be out to tell her you go to Tigger's. So he'd come up and went driving Skiddin' Cat for me. Well, there you could make him much, and one month you had three for Hugo, see. Well, he couldn't blame him. He said, we're wanting to work up there where you're making a lot of money. So he worked up there till camp went down. Then he come down and he worked at this little sawmill here. Francis went out in the woods with a logging crew. Well, his brother -in -law was a run -up. So, there's a community there, of course. The other fella thought Johnny wanted his job. Well, he could have got it, yes.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: But, so, my boy come up from California and he said, he was talking, no, there's a nephew. And he was talking to Johnny about it. He said, come and go back with me, I got a dang good job for you. And Johnny said, well, there's a runner -dozer back in Rome. Johnny said, well, on a run -dozer or something to himself. Now he gets 600 miles. He got a better logging road than this highway head down here through Francis.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Now he got two tanks there to heat his oil. Two scurffiers, three road graders, five dozers, all on. And if he rode to get to that, he could go get any part of the logging crew. So, old Timson got to come and help him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which helps?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: 600 miles of road. A lot of road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, the one thing I was thinking about this when you were, when you were raising a family, was it real hard for you to not be home for a good part of the time and working out?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I was home most of the time. You see, all time the camp was around here. I stayed home, drove back and forth.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And I'm the boss. He took pity on me one winter there. He said, Dick, he said a little too hard on you. He said, run up and down that railroad track night and morning. He said, you come up to camp and stay and I won't charge you a cent lower than old Kelly. So I just went up to camp and stayed.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And I was home. Yeah, it's all, it sure sure changed.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Boy, this timber knife first come here was sure pretty. All those hills in here, all around. Big old yellow pine and tamarack.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So we cut her down in the halter and down the track and then started to load her up the door there at the potlatch.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: She's gone. Boy, the dick was working out and you had to do, you hadn't really raised a family. A lot of clearing.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I had cows to milk and my day consisted of getting him up and packing his lunch pail and sending him to work. And going out and milking the cows and getting the kids up and getting them off to school. And then there was certain times in the year that we did clearing and I'd, that was the hardest though. And just as soon as the kids were gone and the separator washed and the heck with the dishes.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Take off up in the woods and fix up all my fires and pick up and burn and I had a pair of coveralls. And you're working in black wood, you'd just get terrifically dirty, just black. And then I'd work until I heard the four o 'clock whistle blow. And then I'd come down and the kids would be coming home and the girls were old enough and they'd wash up the dishes and sweep the floor. And I would do other things and get the meal ready and they'd wash the supper dishes. I'd take back off up the hill, Dick would be there and the two of us would work until dark.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Clearing?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Burning the stumps out, a lot of them. What did the... Oh, maybe six weeks at a time almost. Have to take a day off now and then to wash. You pack water and wash by hand. Pack water and wash it on the board and if you think the house didn't get to be a mess at such a time. Of course the two older girls did a lot of it. And Peggy helped. She was pretty good to pack water in a gallon bucket.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: That is one of the things I did. And then I always raised a big garden. Which was nothing small, we didn't have irrigation. But we had it over on the creek and we had a steam engine. And we took the hose down into the creek and watered it that way.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Would you like to have a piece of chocolate cake or some cookies?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And that's just about all I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you grow enough of a garden to pretty much take care of the vegetables?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yes, for the family of seven. Well, potato wise perhaps. And some of the other things. I canned everything I could and cabbage I always pitted. And carrots I pitted and I sold quite a lot of stuff to Roy Guernsey's store. And on the way. I even had head lettuce that took down one year and then take it out in trade. Oh, and we always had eggs and during the real depression. If I got those things to town I had to pack them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Walk them in?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Walk them in, yeah. And take two gallon, or two water buckets full of eggs.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And a couple of kids with me. And they could pack a gallon of cream. Sometimes two gallon and they'd switch back and forth.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And drop the eggs off at Princeton and then I had to take the cream onto potlatch. And get, oh we got four cents a dozen for the eggs. It's a long walk. Yeah, and I don't know, maybe we'd get a dollar and a half a bucket for the cream.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: But, oh yeah, and I always bought the kids something if they went and helped. Sometimes the girls, the older girls would take the two buckets of eggs and go to town. And they could always buy a penny candy bar. Which was bigger than a dime when it is now.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: We didn't feel abused though because everybody did the same thing. Everybody did the same thing. We used to have quite a lot of dances during those times.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, we'd move, we had an organ that worked pretty good and they had a lot of them here. Dick had a violin and several others had a violin. And they'd all come and bring some food, you know, whatever they happened to have.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And we'd dance until midnight and then we'd stop and have coffee and whatever there was to eat. And then we'd dance until daylight. And then we'd sit on a 1 by 12 board floor with cracks in it. You know, you can't get them if they're rough. Oh, they're like that far being together. And the men when they were logging, hauling wood, you know, during the Depression, used to get out in the mud. And get their tires all muddy, you know, an awful mess. And I didn't object and they'd bring them in and put them behind the heating stove and let them sit there a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, their tires?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And then you'd get right out here and bounce them up and down and get all the dirt off of them. And then they'd sweep down the cracks.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: They swept it up, but I didn't mind, I never complained. Because I knew how hard it would be with no place to work outdoors and with the mud on them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did dances sound like real togetherness, real neighborliness?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yeah, it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the Depression made people draw closer together?

speaker: Peggy Shott
words: Well, it sure didn't drive them apart. It certainly did. One particular instance, I guess I can talk about it. Oh sure, yeah. One particular instance I remember, we didn't have any coffee. Of course, I was just quite small, but they didn't have any coffee. And our neighbor lady down here in this first house, she's lived there ever since I was about a year old, but didn't have any coffee. So she sent word up saying that they had gotten some coffee. And she had a cup of sugar so she made a cake. And they invited everybody down. Big deal! It was really togetherness and gung ho just like you're having a 50th anniversary now. Big deal. A cake and a cup of coffee. Went down and they drank coffee all afternoon and nobody slept for three days because they hadn't had any coffee. And the caffeine kept them awake and everybody slept for about three days. I mean, now this is togetherness. Everybody drank together and everybody suffered together. But this was, I do remember that. On one Christmas during the Depression we didn't have any money at all, but we sold two cord of wood at Potlatch and got it hauled down and then they measured it up. We don't have electricity. I don't. Hmm. I don't think it's working. I don't think we have any electricity. Oh, I'm not using the electricity. I'm using the batteries. We didn't have anything left at all. You know, just bare, well, we got macaroni and flour and oh, some sugar and I couldn't squeeze. I had to get stockings for the girls. They were going to school. And I just squeezed every penny I could get out of it and bought a fifty cent dollar for Peggy. And the girls never said it yap to yap. But come then Christmas in a couple of days. Of course, I made them doll clothes and things like that. But I didn't know what to have for Christmas dinner. And I didn't have any chocolate. I thought I could make a chocolate cake. And we hadn't had anything except, you know, just plain cake. And so I went down to the neighbors and asked her if she had any chocolate and she'd just gotten a great big box of chocolate. And she gave me a cup of cocoa. And I come home and see there were five of us. And I killed two old hens and stuffed them and cooked them. And we had potatoes and gravy and a canned vegetable. And I made a chocolate pie and put whipped cream on it for supper. And we just had the loveliest Christmas dinner you ever saw. It was just real nice. With nothing else. I don't know, I think I had something that I'd give her in place of the cocoa.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And that's the way it went. And sometimes here would come a peddler from down in the Palouse country. And he'd have, oh, grain or potatoes or something like that, you know. That he'd want to trade for wood. And we'd trade him that for wood. Trade him for a pig.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Or just anything. When you were walking to... But we never, the only time, and I'll tell you about that, that I was ever really worried. We didn't have any flour. We didn't have any sugar. We didn't have any spuds. We had some peas, but they were weavily. And I soaked them overnight and tried to pick all that had weevils out of them and they weren't worth cooking. And we'd eat on to cook.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And Dick and I went up on the hill. It was in the summertime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just what are we going to eat? Just what are we going to do?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: If we kill the chickens, we won't have any eggs or any money. If we eat the eggs, we won't have anything at all to buy anything with. We got milk, but we got to save that cream for a few pennies. And Edgar Adair, a man who was from Princeton, and he had a truck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we were sitting up on the hill just to talk and I was just, what the heck are we going to do?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And I was just, I hadn't had any soap for three weeks. I didn't have any baking powders. I didn't have anything. You know, as far as commodities was concerned. And we just, Dick and I both, just run down over the hill right over the logs and everything and got down there and, Oh boy, he said, I'm glad to see you. He said, I sold, I forget how many cord it was. But he said, I got to take four cord out in groceries.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And if you'll give me eight cord, well I'll let you have the groceries. Well my goodness, you know, beggars can't be choosers. So we got up there and we had an awful lot split. And we just blamed Sue and loaded that car full of that big truckload. And Dick went to town with him and he brought back, he took two cord down.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I don't know, and then I think Edgar got groceries with the other two cord. But you know, for two cord, you could get quite a lot of groceries. We got a barrel of flour, we got 25 pounds of sugar, and we got, oh boy, and we got a lot of soap and we just got everything. And when he came back by, he came back by a farmer, Flansburg, from down in Palouse. And he had an old sow that hadn't pigged that year. And she'd been around out in the pea field. And she was just real nice, not all of that terrible fat. And so Dick bought that from her, traded her for wood. And Flansburg come and got the wood, we brought the sow home with us. Oh, made a whole 50 gallon barrel full, and it was awful nice meat. And then they went and come by, oh, I can't think of the name of the guy that moved this, but he never turned anybody down that went in there. And you take a load of wood in there, and he lets you have flour and feed, and a tank full of gas. And your money's worth for the wood. And Edgar took two cords of wood down for us. So you got two cords worth of groceries and two cords worth of buying power from that wood. And that's because he just made the sale. Oh boy, oh boy, was that ever the, you know, sometimes we get awfully discouraged. But when we look back on it, when we're doing the best we can, and saying a little prayer, and you don't know where, from which way, and from a way you don't expect it the least, therever it comes from. And I used to raise quite a few chickens, and the peddlers would come by here. I had those great big row down reds, and they'd trade me a box of peaches for a chicken, you know, and apples and tomatoes. And just, and that way I could can them up prunes, oh I canned everything I could get my hands on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they kill the chicken and then they'd just take it? They would kill the chicken and then just take it with them?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: They would just take it with them. As a rule. I was on a can about, oh six to eight hundred quart. Not all at once. But you know, when some of the jars were empty. Well then in the fall of the year, there was carrots and cabbage to can. And all that stuff, you know, just everything I could.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And that's kind of the way it helped out too. Of course when you went back to work, you know, after the depression, at the end of the depression, by then it was a considerable easier going. When you say this place in Palouse, which would trade... It wasn't Palouse, it was, it was... Pullman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, it was Colfax, wasn't it?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Pullman, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his name?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Duffy. Duffy. I wonder if that's part of the same duffies that came from Troy. I don't know. Huh. Well, but if he was always there, you could trade with. How come you were in such desperate... Well, sir, we had, didn't have a truck at that time. I don't remember how it was, but we didn't have a truck. No way of hauling anything. There was nothing to do but just make wood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Dick walking to Potlatch when he had work?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: He wasn't working then, there was no work. At that particular time. That's why you were walking into town. Yeah, I didn't have no way to walk, no way to get there. Well, it's too bad that... It was about the same time as they had the... Oh, they put out this work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it? WPA work?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yeah, WPA work. They'd go work a couple of months, a week, a month, and they got $19 a week.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: That was the beginning of the letup.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Dick get on with that? You did get on with that?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And of course, they was... You hated to kill their beef. And there was little government beef running up around the country. Sometimes it would fall and break their neck and we'd use them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Government beef?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had cattle running around the country here?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Government beef are wild critters. Oh, I see, wild. I see. I see. Yeah, well, it happens. Animals do die.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They what?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Wild animals do die. Oh, yeah. Nothing to waste, though. But we'd get it and... I don't know if it would've done any good or not, but then when we'd get it, I'd can it or... something. But anyhow, I canned lots.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people think of leaving, let's say, from around here during the Depression? Or was it just been too rough to go anyplace else?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, we never thought of leaving. Never did. There was no way to get anywhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, automobile?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, I meant, you know, trying your luck somewhere else. But I guess things were probably... Some of them did. Some of them did, but we never thought of such a thing. And you know, my house didn't amount to much. Well, it was two thickness of 1x12 boards inside with tar paper between it. And then I had another baseboard thing that I got before times got so hard and put on top of that, tacked it all over it, but it was heavy like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a baseboard?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I don't know what it was.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: We had that on the house, and the two -by -fours was... There was nothing on the outside. And then there was just a door here, there, and steps up to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then, you know, those little Marion shacks that we bought to build it out of in the first place?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: All the windows we had was in them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you know, they're little squares like this, you know?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: We had one here, and one here, and two back there. And boy, when I first got my windows in, I felt plum -naked! I'm telling you, I really did. But I'd had it without any...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the thingy doing?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: You had it without any windows in it, too. But we got our place paid for. Then we got a well dug, and we got a... lifted the old house up and put it on a foundation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this right here? This house?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And lifted it up and put it on a foundation, and then built on. And finally built a good barn.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Just... It's just up a step at a time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that most everybody else in the neighborhood here was in pretty much the same boat?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Everybody was in the same boat. There was a few men that managed to work at Potlatch, considerable, but Dick was a logger. And so there wasn't much work for him.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Only... We managed to get a payment down on a truck, and he went and took it and went over to Joel, and around over in there, and hauled. To pay for the truck. Hauled green all one fall.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I imagine walking into Potlatch and back must have taken you most of the day. Oh, I was young and I didn't mind it. There was only one time that I remember that it kind of laid me low. But my sister -in -law was going with me. Lola Tribble. And so I got ready to go. And it was muddy. Oh my goodness, it was muddy. And going down that road, it was the hardest walking you ever saw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I was tired when I got there, and she come to the door and said, I got lunch already for us, why don't you come over?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And I said, oh Lola, I'm tired already and I don't want to come over there and then walk back. I think I got a lot to do down there and we better get it going. So she come on down and crossed the bridge and we went to Princeton. And about when we crossed the river bridge there, somebody come along and give us a ride into Princeton. And we had some business to tend to in Princeton. And then we walked on to Potlatch. All the way, and when we got just past the graveyard down there, somebody come along and give us a lift on into Potlatch. And then we had to go to Onaway, we walked over the hill. And we come back and then there was a lumberyard down about where Carter's is. And we had to walk down there and then we had to go back to Onaway and come back over to Potlatch.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And I tell you, I was pretty tired when we got there.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: We had to walk, we walked every step of the way from Potlatch to Princeton. And I got to Princeton and I went in and I sat down like this, a little of a certain miniature on the other side of me. And the man that was teaching school up here, I can't remember his name. No, it was the one after him. And he said, my goodness, you girls look all in. And I said, well I sure am. And he said, I bet you walked all the way to Potlatch and back and didn't get much ride. And we said, we sure did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, well, how many groceries you got to walk home, take home?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And I said, well so many I don't think I'll take them. Well he said, go get them and I'll take you girls home. So he brought us home. And I sure appreciate it. I guess that ordinarily you really couldn't figure you could ask a neighbor to give you a ride that had a car. Well, there wasn't any of them past us. No, there was none of them past us. They just didn't have to be going our way at the right time. None of one of them passed us. But that much Lola and I, either one will never forget. I made more fuss about it than she did, but she stayed in bed all the next day and I didn't.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Oh yeah, I walked to Potlatch and back many times, but that was just real hard going. And the extra we had to walk, I don't remember it's been so long ago.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I think we left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Things that we get together for time after dance after dance?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: No, I don't know whether this is fit to put in there or not, but I felt just like dancing and having one heck of a time. And it was, mosses lived at the house and it's, you go down here to that turn and go right straight back up the hill. It's a little log house up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe you've noticed it, have you?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And they lived there. Well we went to the dance and, boy I went in, we went right to dancing and pretty soon I come out to the sink and this Gail LaRue, he must have been about 16.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And they lived just up this road there. They've done the house over a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, Mae, how much does it take to make you get a man drunk?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Oh, I said, anybody that's any kind of a man could stand a quart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I said, yeah. You feel pretty good about the time you get a quart, you better not try more than that. And I think he never had a drink in his life. And his uncle and another fellow had managed to get about three quarts of whiskey. And they'd had a drink when they first got there. And the kids had found their cash. And Gail downed a quart.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And the other kids got rid of the other quart. And they went, only about that much left in one quart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And were those men ever mad?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Oh, blow me down.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And Violet, Gail's sister, they come in and she was crying, she said, Gail's dead. Gail's dead, he's dead. And he went out there and he was just out like this, just out like a light. He never got to the building anymore. So help me. Oh, and Violet was mad. And Mary was mad. But you hadn't really known that's what he was asking for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know that he never even had a drink before?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know he had the liquor when he said that?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Oh, I had an idea. He found Ben's cash. Because if Ben could get any, he'd get some, you know. And he figured taking the men out after, maybe after supper and giving them a drink apiece. Him and the man that run the company ranch, his name was Hans Peterson at that time. And they're the ones that had the liquor. Oh boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often would there be, would there be dance in those days?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, there was a couple of winters, there was either a pinochle party or a dance every Saturday night. Cost nobody anything except maybe a little food.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far up and down the creek did the families come from? How?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, let's see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know, Gage's wasn't here at that time, were they?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: They came just... No, but Liselles were on the hill, which is two miles. Liselles were on the hill. Two miles that way and two miles down. Yeah, that's about all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how many couples for families would there be, usually?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, let's see, there would be Liselles and Sueders and they didn't come very much. And two families of LaRue's and Weaver's and Browning's and McVeigh's and Moss's and Tribble's and Two Goods and Cars. And Cars didn't always come. And Ross's, I believe that's all. Oh, Jones's. But they all had young people. You know, like Jones's had six kids up here. And then other people flowed in from here and there, too, you know.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: So, it was lots of fun. Oh, Joe Pearson and his family, up over there by Bales's. They used to come. There was three girls and two or three boys. You said a little while ago that you kind of helped raise some of the neighbors' kids, too. Well, I had always had kids and the other kids would come and stay a week. This wasn't at that time particularly, though. No, but later on, or yes and then, too, there was always kids around here.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Always kids around. But as for to take any of them and keep them, I never did. Oh, Wes lived up there, there were quite a few families.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that parents, when you were raising kids, that parents acted very differently than parents do nowadays with their kids?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I haven't been around how they act. With them I've been out of circulation too long. But the girls was going to dances and she was going with this boy and maybe she changed twice during the night. That is, she danced with them and she ate supper with them and she came home with us.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: But they were going together just the same way they thought they were. They were making eyes at one another and holding hands.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Peggy was a little older. We didn't have so many dances here. When Peggy got old enough to grow up, we used to go, there was a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what creek was that on up there where we used to go up to make?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Kenman's. And go to the Flat Creek Schoolhouse. Grouse Creek. Grouse Creek Schoolhouse, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where's the schoolhouse? Was it a log in?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, I know, but then they did have an up on Flat Creek Schoolhouse. Two or three dances I went up to up there with you. That was the Grange Hall, Mom.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the Grange Hall?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: But Peggy was old enough, at least part of that time, to pick her own boyfriend and he'd come and get her. Oh my. Many times she worried me to death. It's not good at events. Well, I think it's interesting how nowadays it seems like parents give their kids a lot of latitude about going on dates and all that. No other way to do it. I had the feeling that in those days a lot of courting was carried on under the watchful eyes of the parents by comparison to today. Well, there again, we didn't have courts. Even when I was in high school, there was no gas available during the war. I mean, if you did have a boyfriend, his folks had a car, once a month is all he could ever get it. So, six or eight or ten kids went together. It wasn't this, it was very, very different. There's no getting around Dad. Just an entirely different aspect on the whole thing. Now if a child, even at 13 or 14, can't get in a car, go from here to Spokane to see a show, they just start living. Whereas us, we would go to one of these parties and great, that's all we, just to run off our energy. My boys, we were very fortunate. We lived out here and they all had horses. They all, then after they graduated from horses, they went to cycles, but we insisted on off -highway bikes. So even I don't understand this, but Mama's working, this is my hang -up, so I shouldn't mention it. Mama's are working, she comes home with work, she's got her own work to do, she is tired, the kids are out of sight, out of mind, to heck with it. In fact, my son -in -law, my daughter -in -law doesn't like to stay home, and he says her home, their home, they lived in law school, Mama worked, Daddy worked, she took care of the kids from the time she was a first grader on. Her younger brothers and sisters got them ready for school, and he said their home was a place to come to when you got hungry and to sleep. And otherwise it was just a house. There was no point in coming home because Mama wasn't there. There was no home there. So this, I shouldn't get involved in that, this is Mama's. One time Peggy gave me some bad time. And it did, we had a car, and there wasn't too much gas available. But this Ernest Hayfield lived up over the hill this way, and he'd been trying to get a date with her and she wasn't too crazy about it. But there was two other girls and two boys come and she said she'd go, and she didn't know the two or not. But she finally went. And I expected them to be home, Dick was working nights, and got home at two o 'clock and I woke up and she wasn't here. I went out in the summertime and got daylight pretty quick and I sat on the steps and I didn't know whether to take off and go straight up or just what to do, or whether the car broke down or they'd run out of gas. And it was just daylight when they come home and I went out there balling and,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you have to do this to me for?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well she said, Mother, the dance statement, we danced till daylight. You said you danced till daylight. It probably must have been about five o 'clock. But all six of them were there yet, they went all together. Oh boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you mad?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Or were you just... No, I wasn't mad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kind of glad to see her again, eh?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And then another time, she was going with this Curly Pummly, and they went to the show in Palouse and they got back as far as Kennedy Ford. And they ran out of gas. But they had quite a bit of time left, and while Peggy says, Daddy gets off at two o 'clock, and so they played around so much and finally began to hurry because it was getting close to that time and they got there and was running right out to try to stop Daddy and he went right on by.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, Geeta, our oldest daughter, was married and lived in Onaway and she went over there. But anyhow, I worried some. And Curly brought her home the next morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: We didn't have telephones, ma 'am. No, we didn't have telephones. So people do have times raising their kids. Not that either time had she really done anything so wrong except that I walked the floor. That's all. And then I wasn't mad. Well, you get too scared to get mad.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When your kids were just little babies and children, were there ways that you were supposed to, things you were supposed to do or not supposed to do with the kids?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I know when I was growing up, a lot of mothers were raising their kids, they said, according to Dr. Spock, he'd come out with some books about child raising and that's how a lot of the mothers were raising their kids where I grew up. You know.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Here again, there weren't the social problems. We didn't have social problems. Really. What we done, we done together. Which didn't leave the children on their own or hardly anything, if you understand what I mean, like the parties, this type of thing. I was playing pinochle with veterans when I was first grader because you grow up with it and you learn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you try to break the kids into working pretty young and training them how to do it?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: You bet ya.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just recently I had a girl come and stay with me and somebody said, well, will May make her work? Will she make her do anything?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And Judy said, huh, I'd just like to see you be around mom and not do any work. Yes, my kids worked.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Always. You better get up and get busy. They're throwing Christmas off. I can't do any more so. Yes, they worked. Johnny worked. And they're all good workers. Every one of them. But there again, we didn't work alone. We weren't sent to do a job particularly. I mean, we went to help with the job. I mean, there's a difference. Send a child out to home in the garden for two hours. It's pretty rough. But if your mom goes with you and works for two hours, there's a difference. There really is a difference. I mean, like if mama goes off to work now and tells her child to stay home and do the dishes and the housework, it's very different than if mom says I'll wash your fuel dry. Yes. Yes.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, you said that you had a run -in with Greybacks once. You said that to me when I was over talking to Dick last time and I was wondering when that time was you had a run -in with Greybacks. Well, we had a cousin. He had a cousin. Johnny Sparks from back east. And he'd been out on the road. And he'd worked a little here and a little there and he'd slept out on the railroad track, you know, in blankets and just where he could. And he'd got Greybacks, which is not unusual.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And he was here a week and he slept in our beds.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And I did wash his clothes. And I stopped having funny looking bugs on his clothes after I washed them. They were dead and he took a bath. But boy, we had Greybacks. And you know, I had to wash all the quilts and all the sheets and all the pillcases and give everybody a bath. And lye soap, we used homemade lye soap. I'm telling you, that was something. I still have homemade soap that I need. And another thing that we did that might be interesting was they got the itch in the school down here.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And if you think that isn't hard to get rid of because you get your kids cured up and the others aren't cured up and they're just, well, wash their hands in the same pan and they got it again. But I used to give them a bath in lye soap and they'd exulfur and lard and rub all over their bodies. And it got rid of it. You know, you go through a lot of things. I think years and years ago they used to have hair lice, but I never had anything to do with that. Oh yes we did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: When you were in Spokane working. I believe and you washed it in coal oil. Grandma would come up and help you. Daddy made me wear a, but we were going to Princeton School. And every now and then the report got out. I don't think I ever had any. But he made us saturate our hair in mineral oil. We had to wear it that way for a week. He wouldn't let us wash it out. Pretty rough. Had to wear a scarf around it, you know. Peggy was a senior in high school. No, I was going to school in Princeton, Mom, at the time. Eighth grader, maybe seventh grade. But anyway, and his cloths would become so saturated with oil during the day and it would run down here. That was pretty rough. I think the lice might have been simpler. No, honey, you couldn't have been in the eighth grade. I was going to school in Princeton at the time. No, you was going to school in Potlatch. You went to school in Potlatch before I went to work at the mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but you worked at the mill and then went to Spokane, remember?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: So it was during this period of time. When you came back I was going to. Anyway, I was in Potlatch. I was going to ask you now about, you mentioned this a little bit before about working at the mill. I was wondering, now the reason that came about is because there was a real shortage during the war.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's why women could go and work in the mill?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what did you think it was like to work there?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, I don't think it hurt me any. Some of the women claimed it did, but I didn't think so. Peggy worked there a year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't think it ever hurt you, did it?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: No, it worked a lot harder as well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the main work that you did at the mill? Would you describe it?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, Peggy never done anything except pick laughs. But I used to pack lumber back over to the green, from the, from the grater back over to the resaw. Or get it there somewhere, whether shove it or pull it or. Anyhow, I done it and cleaned up the mill. But I worked there four years. There were quite a lot of women working there.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: All defense plants pretty much had women working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remember the song, Rosie the Riveter?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: You know, the head of women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was most of the lumber that you were handling stuff that you could carry or did you have to push a lot?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, it come down on chains. Yeah. See, it come from the edger down on chains. And as it went through the trimmer, see, and then over onto the graders. And the grader found some board that needed to be cut down and he'd pull it off. And then I had to get it back over to the edger. And I didn't have too much trouble. I didn't manhandle them. I got me a hook and hooked into it and drug it over. But once in a while, and I was always, never had trouble getting along with men. Because I'd done, you know, I worked hard. But they would be a plank, a tamarack plank, maybe four inches thick and twenty foot long and green.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And do you know anything about timber at all?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, you know how heavy tamarack is. And I couldn't even budge it. And I relieved the men on the trimmer. So I knew how to do that. You know, straightening the boards on the trimmer. And I'd go over there and I'd say, I think you're awful pretty. Gee, you know, I think you're real nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they'd say, now what do you want?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Oh, there's some boards. And so I'd spot while they went and packed the boards back. But they never complained. Or anything. But it just was too much for me. That's all. Sometimes it'd be weeks if there wouldn't be any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the women working at the mill pretty much spend a lot of their time together? Like their break time? Or did everybody make it?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And there was a bed in it. If we wanted to lay down at noon, we could. Or ten minutes at lunch, you know. Time. Or if we got in there early in the morning or something or waiting for our other night. But there was a bed in there for us to lay down. A fairly comfortable bed. And we had water and laboratories and a table and everything. And as far as I know, or at least speaking for myself, the men were good to the women. I never had them trouble with men anyhow.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: We worked over in Moscow 11 years. Three years for girls and the rest of the time for boys. I had a lot of friends in fraternities and a sorority. And I never had... I don't think I ever had any boy ever say anything mean to me. I had hashers in the sorority. And my hashers were just as cooperative as they could be. No trouble at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were cooking?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you find that job, cooking for the college kids? Was it a good job? Did you think that was a good work?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I had been up to Fernwood and I had been working in the mill up there. And then my daughter got ill and it was the end of the season. And she had another baby. And I went down there. And when I come back, I thought, well now I'll just draw my unemployment this winter. And I had cooked in a camp two years up there. I had cooked in camps before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what do you do?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I told them. And I told them and they said, we've got a job for you. The pie -fies sorority up here wants to cook. Or just right after Christmas. Well, you've just got to take it, you know. So that got me started. But my kids were all grown then and I went up to Fernwood where Dick was working the next summer and worked up there. And then in Moscow, in the winter. And I worked in Moscow 11 years. I was up there and I had surgery. And they told me I could go back to work. Boy, I went back to work and I thought it was going to kill me. And I come back home and Doug Schofield was here and wanted me to come over there and cook. And I sure did take it. Because I tell you, handling them boards up there was more than I could. It just about killed me. I just thought I couldn't take it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A potlatch or a fernwood?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: A fernwood, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that heavier work than a potlatch? Or was it about the same?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, it was about the same. Only I had to pack, oh, about eight boards. Just literally pack them up four steps and pile them for another guy to resaw. Which wasn't very good. Or I thought it wasn't. That doesn't sound very good to me. Well, not when you just get out of the hospital. Well, I was thinking about, you know, sometimes... I was wondering if, say, some of those men at the potlatch mill would have had the idea that the women couldn't cut the mustard for doing that, you know, the kind of work that men usually did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there was much of that thinking?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Right at first there was.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: But as long as the women done her work, well... And nobody kicked because I asked them to pack that, because they knew that was too much for me.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And always, even if I was busy and the lumber got to coming awful fast on the chain, well, I'd just fly right in and help them get them straightened out, you know. Always all of those things. And I kept the place clean, slick as a whistle. And... And most of the women were local people that lived on stump ranches. That means a lot of hard work. Clearing land that particular way is very hard work. So most of them did know how hard work was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have an idea of how many women there were working in the mill at a time, say, during the war then?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: About as many as there ever was when you was working there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many was there?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: About.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Thirty? Thirty -five?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Here. I can see in that area. Between twenty -five and forty.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: But Peggy got laid off. Yes, I graduated and... She took her money and went to Moscow and spent it all on a suit and a hat and shoes and got married. Furniture, Grandma. I bought a lot of furniture. And furniture, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many women not stick with it or did most of the people stick with it when they started at the mill?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yeah, most of them stuck with it. A few of them. Oh, like one girl I knew she wanted a rug and she was a good worker. Boy, she'd come up there and she just worked to beat the band. And then she got money enough to buy her a rug and a new dinette set and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what I mean?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: The wages were really... I bought low -weight cables for sixty cents an hour. It worked down there for... Oh, six... must have been less than that. I started at...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it sixty -five cents an hour?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I don't know. Eighty -five. I mean, which was really top -weight, just the same as five and a half or six and a half would be now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A dollar sixty -five, you mean?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: It may have been a dollar sixty -five. So you really, you could build your house, you built the house by working there. You say you just about built the house by working there. Yeah.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: So you can work pretty hard, you know, work extra hard for that much money, comparatively. Well, that is, I dug the well and put in the water and then...

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: fixed the bedroom and the bathroom in here and... and then went forty -two hundred dollars in debt and built the rest of it. What it's paid for. There was more than that went into it, too. Well, this land query... See now, it is from that there. On over. See, and I started in from the middle of the house and went out. So, see, I have lots of room upstairs. And it gets tickled at my husband. And when I first went to work the mill, soon as I can get enough money ahead, I'm gonna... put a great big window in here... and I'm gonna put the door on this side. Oh, no, you do that and the house will fall down for sure. And the other day something happened and he told me, well, see there now the house is all going out of shape. I told you it would. I put that in first and put the windows in. You know, in the bathroom and all that. And then I built from that on out on the foundation around it. The upstairs and the fireplace.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: The fireplace cost $600. I wonder just what it would cost now. A great deal more, Jan, at least, I imagine. Because it is a nice fireplace.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And Dick just loves the windows, especially this one. He calls it his hunting window.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And I like my house, every bit of it. It's a beautiful house. It's not as good as if it had all been built at once, you know. I hardly noticed it. I would never have known it if you didn't tell me. Really, it was in parts. A lot of houses are like that.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: You were saying before that... We were talking a little bit about whether you work for yourself or work for somebody else. And, you know, some people like one or the other. You were saying pretty much that you think they're just kind of better off working, not working for themselves. I don't know. Dick always worked out. Now, Herman's father worked out very little. And Herman didn't work out until he got his place where he was born and his kids raised.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that right, Peggy?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Very, very little. Maybe just a little once in a great while. The world there has changed too. The general attitude of people. It is so terribly expensive to get set up in any kind of a business. When we first started farming, that's not been that long ago. We didn't have any money. So, we'd use old bed springs. We'd go along in our pickup and spread the seed. We'd use old bed springs for Harold. Well, nowadays this would be out of the question. It would be impossible.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: So it depends on how bad you want to work for yourself. If you can stand the guff of starting out, it's very difficult. Well, there isn't any of our children that could have taken over the farm. It just wasn't in the cards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much land do you have now?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Four hundred and forty.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: But that's beside the matter. It's the individual person. My husband just seems as an individual but says if somebody else can run a business, there's no reason why I can't run it and make my profit in his too. This is his attitude but this has changed now. I mean, as you well know, if you were going out and going into the business for yourself, it would be very, very difficult, even without interest, to find the money to back you. So, I think our young people have turned to education to improve themselves rather than trying to start their own business. Not that it's impossible but far more difficult than when we were starting. But you said Dick did try farming once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say Dick did farm once for a few years?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: About two years. He's no farmer. In the last year he farmed, he went eight hundred dollars in the home. He is just not a farmer. It's a matter of working for yourself. When you work for someone else for so many, many years, unless you have a dream of doing something for yourself, it's a matter of putting in the hours. Then you get your money and after you've done this for so many, many years, you come home, unless there's someone to oversee you and to tell you to go ahead, it's hard to get the job done. You put in your twelve hours but the job isn't done. This is not being critical. No way. No, it's the way you've gone about your working career. This is the habit you've developed. Johnny farmed one year and he raised a good crop. But I blow Dick out once. He had a thirty cat. And it takes a lot of gas. And he had a team here. But he had three sections of harrows which the team would have dug. And he was going over the ground with that cat.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: It just didn't pay. It was a good day.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: When we talk about it today, Dick was saying he preferred, you would prefer the wood work. Oh yeah, he always liked it. He thought he liked to farm but he didn't. And we was milking cows and when he started farming them, I got time to fool with him and he turned them all out.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I was working at the mill at the time. And he should have been able to make it good but he didn't. In my opinion it all boils down to the theory that if you're working for yourself, you take care of all the little jobs. See that all the little jobs are done. The big jobs take care of themselves. When it's hang time it gets done. But the little jobs, the fixing the fence, the little jobs, if you take care of them, the big ones take care of themselves. And someone that's worked out for wages or really don't see the little jobs take care of the big jobs and the little jobs kill you. I mean they collapse the, it collapses the big jobs. But Dick was a, he was a master logger. He was very, very, very good at his job. He knew just, well an excellent logger.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And people always come, always in late years when it's changed so much, he probably wouldn't be so invaluable. Come and look over this timber for me and lay where you think I should put the roads. And do this and do that. And he was a very, very excellent farmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Electricity's off. Hot water doesn't have any either. Well our clock says, oh that's right, they couldn't have any school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Happy kids?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Not bad. So Dick was, really had a fine reputation. Doesn't surprise me at all because he certainly, he certainly seems to know logging from one end to the other. Yeah, and he's a good logger and he understands it and he knows how to take advantage of it. He knows what log is and he's just real good at it. That's just the truth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me a little more about how this lamp clearing, that you would do to get it?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Sounds like quite a job and one that never went. Oh after we got the steam engine, it wasn't so bad. But I tell you, this hillside up here, I know I worked it all one spring. And like I say, I put on coveralls, cover, you know, completely. And then when I'd come home, I'd come back to the house, I'd turn them wrong side out and come in and put cold cream on my face. I couldn't wash my face because there was so much black, you know, so much, that lie in there. But that was hard work. Pick up and pull it over and pile it together and chop it up and... Oh I think one spring when it got... When I had to quit because it got too dry. I counted 63 stumps. As I could stand down in and they would come up to here.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And... And little kids would pick up little pieces, I remember that. Yeah, you betcha. Betcha was fine. Johnny up there one time, it was hot, it was getting kind of hot one Saturday. Some other boys was over there on the creek playing and I had all the kids up there picking up sticks. The older girls was in the house doing, getting supper and doing the ironing and things that had to be done. Johnny and Stephanie look over to the creek and... Dick was up there with it because it happened to me on Saturday. Well come on here, get that up, get that up, pick that up here, Judy, you get this. And Peggy never had to be told. She just piled right into it. And he looked over there and he said, Oh dear. And there was a dog laying in the shade of a log. And then he said, I wish I was a dog. I wish I was a dog.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: But he learned to drive the cat when he was quite young and... He then went to work for other farmers and... Two years he worked for Waltzer's, Hugo Waltzer down here. And it made good money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before you married Dick and came up here, where was it that you lived before?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well I was born in Pomeroy, Washington. And then my mother died when I was 18 months old. And then my grandparents took me up to Winchester. And I was there until I was about 7. Then we moved to Lewiston. And I was in Lewiston until I was 18. 17.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Until I was 17. And then I was 22 when I married Dick.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: What did you think of Hattercrick when you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What? What did you think of this area when you first moved over here?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well I... I came up and got a job as a hash... You know, as a working in the logging plant in the cook shack, you know. Waiting tables. And oh, I was just busy as a bee, you know, just flying around there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And this guy sitting over there, he smiled at me so nice and said, Is there any warm hotcakes?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And I looked him over and he was 30. And I thought, hmm, boy I bet he's good to his wife. And it went right off the waiting table. Then I had a blind date. And it turned out to be him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I looked at him and I said, Oh, so you're the one they call Dick Benj, are you?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And he said, part of the time.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And my goodness, I couldn't get rid of him. I couldn't get rid of him. He was right there, and I turned around. He must have taken a liking to you. I evidently did because he was right there all the time.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: He didn't have any, he was more of a lumberjack style. And he didn't have anything the first two times they took me out, except a new pair of big overhauls and a clean shirt. One time he wore a pair of Malone pants. And the next time he'd come to get me, he had a brand new suit. And his tie was on crooked. Bless his heart.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yeah. His tie was on crooked. I was going to ask you about church too, around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were first married and with the little kids, was there a community church in Princeton at that time?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, yes. But then they meant mostly to the Nazarene church. Because the Nazarene minister would come out and get them. And I didn't go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that terrible?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Not at all. Yes. I wish I would have, but I didn't. And then after I became a Christian, I prayed so hard that... My kids went to Bible school. They weren't completely ignorant and they went to Sunday school. That they would come to the Lord, somehow or other. And they did. Peggy joined the Catholic church. Judy joined the Lutheran. Johnny joined the Baptist. And Gita joined the Christian. Johnny's a Mason now. Johnny's a Mason now. And we have one sister that is a Mormon. One sister that borns the first Christian. Religion is very seldom discussed at a family gallery. And Claire Bell joined the Mormon church. Mormon, Catholic, Lutheran, Christian, Mason.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that should be a lesson for me, shouldn't it?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yes. Yes. It doesn't matter where they come as long as they belong to the Lord.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: We just don't talk. We just don't discuss it too much. We don't avoid it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: We don't avoid it, no. We don't have any discussions about it either. We don't avoid this, I think. It sounds like the family though is still kind of close. Oh, you never find a family that's closer. You never could find a family that's any closer. Or that stick together when anything happens to one. They're right there. In fact, my sister is probably the best friend. I've ever had Judy, the one that was here when you were here before. We're just real good friends. I don't think I've ever had a better friend.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sister?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yeah. Doesn't make a better difference. She is my friend. We have always been very close. My husband and her are especially close. Now this sounds ridiculous because they're not related. I mean, but they're just exceptionally close. I don't think... Be the one of them ever needed help. I'm sure that's just right where they go. And this is great. This is real great. He's far closer to her than he is to any of his sisters. And she is far closer to him than her brother. We're close to my brother, but family -wise, we don't know him anymore as a friend. Let's put it this way. He's our brother, sure. We love him, but we don't know him as a person because he's been in California for 15 years. And as a friend, we know he's there if we need him. But distance has lessened this friendship type thing. When Judy was growing up, you know, and starting to go with the boy, she was two years younger than Johnny. And boy, he kept an eagle eye on her. I'm telling you. One time, on Princeton, the laboratories was out behind the house. And she come and asked me if she could go out. I said, well, come right back. And I was helping back in the kitchen, you know. And I guess she didn't even get that far because there was some boys out there started hollering at her, you know. And Johnny just went and grabbed her around the waist and brought her in the house and stood her down.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: She bawled a while and I just thought, well, let him handle it.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And very soon I looked around. There was out on the floor dancing. And if she was going with a new boy that he wasn't too sure about, well, he'd follow him all the way home. And back there's and wait until she come in the house.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you compare that your family is as close as ours and the families used to be in the early days?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: It seems to me that families used to be a lot closer on average maybe than they are. Well, we're all close together when we're together, but the three of us are here. And then Claire's cleared down in lower California and Johnny's up on Arcata. We don't see Claire so very much. She's got six kids and she's got about all she can handle. I think it isn't. My personal opinion is that every, I mean, we have strived so much, psychology has, to become individuals. They strive for this from the time you're in the first grade on. Become an individual. Do your own thing. Do what you, you know, this type of thing. And families are close, but even get in school and each individual goes their own way. Well, we used to all go the same way. You know, to a degree. I'm not saying it's good or bad, but I think it has done a lot to destroy, not destroy. It doesn't encourage this family unit closest. Our family, of course, this is, if you don't mind, we were just very fortunate. Herman and the boys are so very, very close. But we're just, uh... And they're all together yet. Oh, they're just, well, we all live locally, but they're all home at least once a week. And even Herman down here, they all come at least once a week and sometimes twice a week. And this is, but Herman is real close.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that maybe most of the families were, tended to be real close in the, like in the early days when you were growing up? Do you think families were a lot closer then than most of them are nowadays?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Well, see, I only had one half -sister. And my grandparents raised me. And of course, in the summer when school was out, we'd go around and visit all summer to each one of the boys. You know, and she had seven sons and a daughter. And my father married again and we'd go and visit every one of them. Oh, stay a week, ten days, and maybe with my Aunt Ella. Longer than that. So I got to know all of my cousins real well. And I guess the family was close. They never had a great many family gatherings. There were nearly always some of them there. And then the ones that lived close would come for Christmas dinner, but they never come very far to come to a big dinner. I try to have Thanksgiving dinner, but I get, have pretty good luck. They pretty much come and you can't insist on if it was your kids, but your grandkids kind of get tired of going up to the old folks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They don't act it, but then, they pretty much come, don't they Peggy? Do you think transportation in those days did alter the fact that if they moved away, that they were disclosed then?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Johnny has been up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He came up in August, wasn't it?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: He was here in January.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or the reunion?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: That was in the summer, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: They've been up once since. No, I mean, yeah, when you were growing up, you think transportation did alter the... Oh, yes. The family togetherness type thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, when the school was here, did you have much to do with the school?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Did parents,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: were they very much involved in it?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: No, the only thing is that I gave dances down here in the schoolhouse about four of them to get money to buy, when we had the log schoolhouse down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To get money to buy what?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Oh, seats and things, some more blackboard and things for the school. And then, the only other thing I'd done was when they were consolidating and the board said, we cannot afford a bus, we just can't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, I think you were married then, weren't you?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Just about. And I put on a small half -hour play and two smaller ones. And then they had a basket social and a dance. And then, when we had that, I got up and announced that if I could get enough volunteers that wanted to be in it, that we would put on a full -length play. And maybe we could raise the rest of the money for the bus. We put it on three times.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: But I never worked with a group of children or a group of young people that was so willing and worked together so good. And they, of course, now they were all high schoolers. And they would stop as they came home on the bus, most of them was on the bus. And I would be there and have the hall warm. And we'd practice until about six thirty or seven. And then they took turns bringing food, you know, and we'd eat our dinner and wash our dishes. And then practice until nine. And that wasn't so late, but the kids could go home and do some homework if they had to. About three nights a week.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: And we put it on three times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, do you remember what play it was?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Oh yeah, Object Matrimony. And there were twelve children in it. I don't know, the one down here, it was a two and a half hour play. And we had something in between each play. There was three acts and it was all one scene.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what was Object Matrimony about? Sounds like it was about marriage, would that be a big yes?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Object Matrimony. Well they were these girls, these six girls, and there was no boys around or anything. And they was wanting boyfriends and they didn't know them, so one of the boys come in that was a reporter there and he said, Let me put it in the paper.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: I don't remember what the ad was, but then he put it in the paper and then these guys showed up. And Clara was the cook, and she was an older girl. And Johnny was an old soldier, dressed up in an old soldier suit that would come courting her.

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Everybody got married in the end.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was cute wasn't it honey?

speaker: Ella Mae Arden Benge
words: Yeah it was, a lot of work. And it raised a fair bit of money then. It raised the money. What hall was this in the school house that you put the... It was on the Princeton Hall. The Greenstone. That's great. Sounds really like fun. This wasn't the first time you had done this too. Other high school kids wouldn't have anything to do in the winter. And like I said, gas was at a premium. It wasn't available during the war. We had stamps to get gas when the stamps were gone. That was it.
